experiment: BC3b_circuit_v2 — Priority Arbiter FPL test (properly pre-registered replication of c17)
description: Properly pre-registered replication of the exploratory BC3b circuit result (c17, run May 22, 2026 without following the pre-registration workflow). This experiment tests whether the Functional Proximity Law holds in a 4-channel priority interrupt arbiter (19 nodes) — a combinational logic circuit implementing the same interrupt masking and priority resolution logic as the ISCAS85 c432 benchmark, scaled to 4 channels. The theoretical prediction follows directly from the BC3b mechanism: pure boolean algebra denies FPL because d1/d2/d3 all collapse to logical consequence (layer degeneracy). Physical circuits do NOT exhibit this collapse because fan-in/fan-out topology, structural neighborhood, and timing propagation are structurally distinct regimes. The arbiter dataset has: 8 primary inputs (req0-3, mask0-3), 11 combinational gates (4 AND masking gates mreq0-3, 3 NOT blocking gates inv1-3, 3 AND grant gates ch0-2, 1 OR output irq). Circuit depth = 4. Critical path: req3/mask3 -> mreq3 -> inv3 -> ch0/ch1/ch2 -> irq (12 distinct paths of length 4, all critical). d1 hub: ch0 (degree=5) — the lowest-priority grant gate requires the most wiring inputs (4 inputs: mreq0, inv3, inv2, inv1). Exploratory result from c17 (n=11): r(gate_wiring, structural_proximity)=0.7504 (p=0.01, large effect) vs r(gate_wiring, critical_path_coupling)=0.2813 (ns). This experiment pre-registers the same FPL claim for a DIFFERENT, larger circuit to replicate the c17 finding independently.

hypotheses.statement: FPL directional inequality holds: r(gate_wiring, structural_proximity) > r(gate_wiring, critical_path_coupling). Gate_wiring (d1) and structural_proximity (d2) both encode physical topology — d1 captures direct signal connections, d2 captures neighborhood at distance<=2. Both layers reflect the same structural regime: combinational fan-in/fan-out. critical_path_coupling (d3) encodes timing behavior — which nodes co-occur on the longest propagation paths. The priority chain structure (inv3 blocks ch2/ch1/ch0; inv2 blocks ch1/ch0; inv1 blocks ch0) creates a timing hierarchy distinct from the local wiring topology. Therefore r(d1,d2) > r(d1,d3). This replicates the c17 exploratory finding (r=0.7504 vs 0.2813) in an independent, larger circuit (n=19 vs n=11) that models interrupt priority resolution — the same functional domain as ISCAS85 c432.

r(gate_wiring, structural_proximity) is statistically significant (p < 0.05) with a large effect size (r >= 0.5). The circuit has n=19 nodes with Var(d1)=2.152, providing adequate statistical power. The d1 hub ch0 (degree=5) is also the d2 hub (degree=12), indicating strong topological alignment between layers that should produce a significant positive r. This is the key quantitative claim distinguishing physical circuits from BC3b pure-boolean-algebra collapse (where r->0 due to layer degeneracy).

Hub identity is preserved across d1 and d2: the rank-1 hub in gate_wiring (ch0, degree=5) is also rank-1 or rank-2 in structural_proximity. ch0 is the lowest-priority grant gate AND(mreq0, inv3, inv2, inv1) — it has the most wiring inputs (4 direct connections from 3 inversion gates + 1 masking gate) and therefore the largest structural neighborhood (it is within wiring distance 2 of most other nodes). This tests that hub identity is preserved between the structural coupling layers d1 and d2, as required by FPL.

The d3 hub (critical_path_coupling) is DIFFERENT from the d1 hub: the rank-1 node in critical_path_coupling is irq (the OR output gate) or inv3 (the high-priority inversion gate), NOT ch0. irq appears in all 12 critical paths as their terminal node (degree=15 in d3). This hub displacement from d1 (ch0) to d3 (irq or inv3) is precisely what causes r(d1,d3) to be lower than r(d1,d2): the timing behavior elevates the terminal output node (irq) and the priority bottleneck (inv3) as hubs, while the wiring topology elevates the lowest-priority grant gate (ch0) as hub. This finding would be IMPOSSIBLE in a pure boolean algebra BC3b setting (where all layers collapse to logical consequence and no hub distinction exists).
